title: Americas
subtitle: Pre-Columbian builders, painters, and potters — Mexico to the Andes.
intro: Cities larger than any in contemporary Europe, pyramid complexes aligned to the stars, and some of the finest portrait pottery ever made — all built without iron, wheel, or draft animals.

works.title: Olmec Colossal Heads
works.meta: Olmec · 1200–400 BCE · Gulf coast, Mexico
works.desc: Seventeen basalt heads, up to ten feet tall, each with a distinct face. Carved with stone tools and floated on rafts from the quarry, 60 miles away.

works.title: Teotihuacan
works.meta: Pre-Aztec · 100 BCE – 550 CE · Mexico
works.desc: A city of over 100,000 at its peak — larger than Rome at the same time. Already abandoned when the Aztecs found it and named it "the place where gods were born."

works.title: Tikal
works.meta: Classic Maya · 400 BCE – 900 CE · Guatemala
works.desc: Step pyramids rising above the rainforest canopy. A Maya superpower that swallowed rival cities until a long drought took it down.

works.title: Palenque
works.meta: Classic Maya · 226 BCE – 799 CE · Chiapas
works.desc: A small Maya kingdom with exquisite stucco portraits and one of the most carefully decoded royal tombs in the Americas — that of Pakal the Great.

works.title: Moche Culture
works.meta: Moche · 100–700 CE · Northern Peru
works.desc: Before the Inca, the Moche built painted pyramids and made portrait pots so individual you can tell one person from another across fifteen hundred years.

works.title: Nazca Lines
works.meta: Nazca culture · 500 BCE – 500 CE · Peru
works.desc: Hummingbirds, monkeys, and spiders drawn at the scale of football fields by clearing pebbles from the desert floor. Visible only from the air.

works.title: Chichen Itza
works.meta: Post-Classic Maya · 600–1200 CE · Yucatán
works.desc: On the spring equinox, sunlight strikes the pyramid's stairway to form a serpent that seems to slither down the temple of Kukulkan.

works.title: Coatlicue
works.meta: Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
works.desc: "She of the Serpent Skirt" — the earth goddess, severed neck bleeding two snakes, wearing a necklace of hands and hearts. Found under Mexico City in 1790.

works.title: Aztec Sun Stone
works.meta: Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
works.desc: A basalt disk over three meters across, carving the five successive suns of Aztec cosmology. Buried under the Zócalo in Mexico City until 1790.

works.title: Sacsayhuamán
works.meta: Inca · 15th c. · Cusco, Peru
works.desc: A fortress-temple above Cusco built from stones weighing up to 200 tons, fitted so precisely a sheet of paper won't pass between them.

works.title: Machu Picchu
works.meta: Inca · 15th c. · Peru
works.desc: A royal estate on a knife-edged ridge. The stones of its walls are cut so precisely that no mortar holds them — and they have survived five centuries of earthquakes.

works.title: Monte Albán
works.meta: Zapotec · 500 BCE – 850 CE · Oaxaca
works.desc: A leveled mountaintop city with one of the earliest writing systems in the Americas. Its danzantes reliefs show contorted, stripped captives.

works.title: Copán
works.meta: Classic Maya · 400–820 CE · Honduras
works.desc: A small Maya kingdom with the finest stone sculpture in the Americas. Stelae-portraits of kings carved nearly in the round.

works.title: Mesa Verde Cliff Palace
works.meta: Ancestral Pueblo · 12th–13th c. · Colorado
works.desc: Villages tucked under sandstone overhangs. The Ancestral Puebloans built, lived in, and abandoned them in a little over a century.

works.title: Tiwanaku
works.meta: Tiwanaku · 500–1000 CE · Bolivia
works.desc: A highland capital on the shore of Lake Titicaca. Its Gate of the Sun — cut from a single stone — faces the point where the sun rises at equinox.

works.title: Chavín de Huántar
works.meta: Chavín · 900–200 BCE · Peru
works.desc: A mountain temple with underground galleries and the Lanzón, a carved granite blade — Peru's oldest pan-Andean god, still in his original chamber.

works.title: Chan Chan
works.meta: Chimú · 900–1470 CE · Peru
works.desc: The largest adobe city in the Americas. Twelve miles of mud-brick walls carved with rows of seabirds, fish, and waves.

works.title: Atlantean Figures of Tula
works.meta: Toltec · 10th–12th c. · Mexico
works.desc: Fifteen-foot basalt warriors in feathered headdresses on top of Pyramid B. They held up the roof of a temple that is long gone.

works.title: Cueva de las Manos
works.meta: Patagonian hunter-gatherers · 7300–700 BCE · Argentina
works.desc: A cave with hundreds of human hand stencils, mostly left hands — because the artists were holding the bone tube of pigment in the right, blowing around it.

works.title: Cahokia Mounds
works.meta: Mississippian · 1050–1350 · Illinois
works.desc: The largest city in North America before Europeans arrived — bigger than London in its day. Monks Mound alone is a hundred feet tall, built of earth by hand.

works.title: Serpent Mound
works.meta: Fort Ancient or Adena · c. 1070 CE · Ohio
works.desc: A quarter-mile-long effigy mound of a snake swallowing an egg. The head aligns with sunset at the summer solstice; the coils seem to encode lunar cycles.

works.title: Northwest Coast Totem Poles
works.meta: Haida, Tlingit, Kwakwaka'wakw · 18th–20th c.
works.desc: Cedar poles carved with family lineage, clan animals, and narrated stories. Each is a library — readable top to bottom by those who know the figures.

works.title: Navajo Weaving
works.meta: Diné · 17th c. onward · American Southwest
works.desc: Tightly woven wool rugs and wearing blankets — the weaver puts a deliberate flaw into each one, a ch'į́įdii line, so the spirit of the piece has a way out.

works.title: Chaco Canyon
works.meta: Ancestral Pueblo · 850–1250 · New Mexico
works.desc: A ceremonial center of "great houses" — multi-story sandstone buildings in a treeless canyon. Roads radiate out straight for sixty miles, aligned to the sky.

works.title: Taos Pueblo
works.meta: Tiwa · 1000+ years continuous · New Mexico
works.desc: Adobe pueblos stacked five stories high. Continuously inhabited longer than any community in the U.S. — the family on the top floor climbs ladders up and down.

works.title: Quimbaya Goldwork
works.meta: Quimbaya · 300–1550 CE · Colombia
works.desc: Cast gold figures of caciques, poporos, and seated shamans. In 1892 a diplomat gave 122 of them to Spain — one of the largest pre-Columbian collections anywhere.

works.title: Lord of Sipán Tomb
works.meta: Moche · c. 290 CE · Peru
works.desc: The intact grave of a Moche warrior-priest, draped in gold, silver, turquoise, and Spondylus. Discovered in 1987 moments ahead of looters.

works.title: Spirit of Haida Gwaii
works.meta: Haida · Bill Reid · 1986–1991
works.desc: A bronze canoe filled with thirteen mythic figures paddling together — Raven, Bear, Eagle, Wolf, and others. Reid worked on it for five years while Parkinson's overtook him.

works.title: Maria Martinez Blackware
works.meta: San Ildefonso Pueblo · 20th c. · New Mexico
works.desc: Maria and her husband Julian revived and reinvented matte-on-black pueblo pottery. Their pots sit in every major American museum — and still on shelves at San Ildefonso.

works.title: Ghost Dance Shirt
works.meta: Lakota · 1890 · Great Plains
works.desc: Hide shirts painted with stars and eagles, worn in the Ghost Dance — believed to turn aside bullets. Shirts were collected from the dead at Wounded Knee.

works.title: Pyramid of the Magician
works.meta: Late Classic Maya · 600–900 CE · Uxmal
works.desc: An oval-based pyramid whose legend says it was built in one night by a dwarf magician. Every stone is carved; the central doorway is a monster's open mouth.

works.title: Tumi Ceremonial Knife
works.meta: Lambayeque · 900–1100 · Northern Peru
works.desc: A gold sacrificial knife topped with the long-eared god Naylamp, eyes inlaid with turquoise. Used for ritual decapitation; now a symbol of Peru on souvenir shelves everywhere.

works.title: Coyolxāuhqui Stone
works.meta: Mexica · late 15th c. · Tenochtitlan
works.desc: A twelve-foot disk of the moon goddess, dismembered by her brother Huitzilopochtli. Found by electric-company workers in Mexico City in 1978 — it launched modern excavation of the Templo Mayor.

works.title: Paquimé (Casas Grandes)
works.meta: Mogollon · 13th–14th c. · Chihuahua, Mexico
works.desc: Multi-story adobe pueblos north of the Maya world but connected to them — Paquimé traded in parrots, copper bells, and turquoise. Burned and abandoned before the Spanish arrived.

works.title: Crooked Beak of Heaven Mask
works.meta: Kwakwaka'wakw · 19th c. · Pacific Northwest
works.desc: A transformation mask from the Hamatsa winter dance. The outer "cannibal bird" beak opens with strings during the dance to reveal a human face within — the return of the initiate.

works.title: Hopi Katsina Doll
works.meta: Hopi · 19th c. onward · American Southwest
works.desc: Carved cottonwood figures given to Hopi children to teach them the 400+ spirit beings of the Katsinam. Collectors later prized them so much the carving style changed to please buyers.

works.title: Zuni Fetishes
works.meta: A:shiwi (Zuni) · traditional · New Mexico
works.desc: Small stone carvings of animals — bear, eagle, mountain lion — each holding a spirit and often wrapped with an offering bundle of turquoise or shell.

works.title: Inuit Art
works.meta: Inuit · 20th c. contemporary · Arctic
works.desc: Carved soapstone, serpentine, and whalebone figures of hunters, spirits, and animals. A tradition fused out of older ivory carving and 20th-century print cooperatives like Cape Dorset.

works.title: Haida Argillite Carving
works.meta: Haida · 19th c. onward · Haida Gwaii
works.desc: Black shale carvings only the Haida may quarry, developed as a trade art for European sailors. Ship figureheads, pipes, and eventually complex narrative panels.

works.title: Bonampak Murals
works.meta: Classic Maya · c. 790 CE · Chiapas
works.desc: Three rooms painted wall to ceiling: a royal presentation, a battle, a victory dance ending in the torture of captives. The most complete Maya narrative painting to survive.

works.title: Dresden Codex
works.meta: Late Post-Classic Maya · 11th–12th c.
works.desc: One of only four surviving Maya codices — 39 sheets of bark paper folded like a screen. Astronomical tables of Venus cycles, eclipses, and the rains. Almost everything else was burned by Spanish friars.

works.title: Ollantaytambo
works.meta: Inca · 15th c. · Sacred Valley
works.desc: Fortress and town at the end of the Sacred Valley. Its huge terrace walls are fitted together in the same mortarless style as Machu Picchu — and in 1537 its defenders held off Pizarro's cavalry from them.

works.title: Paracas Textile
works.meta: Paracas · c. 300–200 BCE · Peru
works.desc: Embroidered mummy bundles wrapped in layer after layer of cotton and camelid fiber — winged shamans, deities, severed heads — colors still bright after two millennia in the Paracas desert.

works.title: Codex Mendoza
works.meta: Early colonial Mexican · 1541–1542
works.desc: Aztec tribute lists and imperial history commissioned for Charles V. Captured by French pirates en route to Spain, ended up in Oxford's Bodleian. The most detailed document we have of Aztec administration.

works.title: Calakmul
works.meta: Classic Maya · 5th–8th c. · Campeche
works.desc: One of the two great Maya superpowers (with Tikal). A kingdom of 50,000 in a rainforest, its two largest pyramids rise 45 meters above the canopy.

works.title: Hiawatha Belt
works.meta: Haudenosaunee · c. 1500 · Northeast Woodlands
works.desc: A wampum belt recording the founding of the Iroquois Confederacy: five nations joined by a central tree. Belts were both treaty and record — texts read by running fingers across the beads.

works.title: San Agustín Stone Figures
works.meta: San Agustín culture · 100 BCE – 800 CE · Colombia
works.desc: Over 500 monolithic statues of guardians, jaguar-men, and birth scenes, scattered across an Andean plateau. Who the carvers were, or why they stopped, remains unknown.

works.title: Muisca Raft (El Dorado)
works.meta: Muisca · 600–1600 CE · Colombia
works.desc: A miniature gold raft showing the chief and his attendants during the initiation ritual in Lake Guatavita — the story that grew into the European myth of El Dorado.

works.title: Chimor
works.meta: Chimú · 900–1470 CE · Northern Peru
works.desc: Kingdom that ruled the Peruvian coast before the Inca conquered it. Its capital Chan Chan is the largest adobe city ever built; gold, silver, and feather work survive in elite tombs.

works.title: El Castillo, Chichen Itzá
works.meta: Maya-Toltec · c. 900–1200 CE · Yucatán
works.desc: A step pyramid whose 91 steps on each of four sides plus the temple platform equal 365. On the spring and autumn equinoxes, the sun throws a serpent-shaped shadow slithering down the balustrade toward carved snake heads at the base.

works.title: El Caracol
works.meta: Maya-Toltec · c. 900 CE · Chichen Itzá
works.desc: A round astronomical observatory whose upper windows align with the extreme positions of Venus — a planet so important to the Maya that their calendars track it to the day. The ruined tower earned its name ('the snail') from its internal spiral staircase.

works.title: Pyramid of the Sun
works.meta: Teotihuacan · c. 200 CE · Mexico
works.desc: The third-largest pyramid in the world by volume, built over a natural cave that was probably the origin myth's portal to the underworld. The builders of Teotihuacan are unknown — the city had fallen five centuries before the Aztec arrived to rename it.

works.title: Pyramid of the Moon
works.meta: Teotihuacan · c. 200 CE · Mexico
works.desc: Smaller than the Pyramid of the Sun but terminating the Avenue of the Dead in its shadow of Cerro Gordo. Archaeologists tunneling into it have found successive burial caches with jade figurines, obsidian blades, and sacrificed wolves.

works.title: Tula
works.meta: Toltec · c. 900–1150 CE · Hidalgo, Mexico
works.desc: Capital of the Toltec — probably the 'Tollan' the Aztec considered their civilized predecessors. Colossal warrior atlantes 4.6 meters tall, bristling with atlatl spear-throwers and pectorals of stylized butterflies, still stand atop Pyramid B.

works.title: El Tajín
works.meta: Classic Veracruz · c. 600–1200 CE · Mexico
works.desc: Its 'Pyramid of the Niches' has exactly 365 recessed square niches, one for each day of the solar year. Seventeen ball courts punctuate the site — carved with scenes of ritual decapitation that followed the game's conclusion.

works.title: Tulum
works.meta: Late Post-classic Maya · c. 1200–1500 CE · Yucatán
works.desc: A small Maya port perched on twelve-meter cliffs above the Caribbean, walled on three landward sides. The Temple of the Descending God shows an inverted deity plunging head-first — probably the bee god so central to Yucatán cosmology.

works.title: Uxmal
works.meta: Puuc Maya · c. 850 CE · Yucatán
works.desc: Puuc-style architecture at its best, with mosaic façades of thousands of perfectly cut limestone panels forming rain god masks. The Pyramid of the Magician has uniquely rounded sides and the locals say it was raised in a single night by a dwarf.

works.title: Mitla
works.meta: Late Zapotec · 1200–1500 CE · Oaxaca
works.desc: Religious capital of the Zapotec after the fall of Monte Albán, whose façades are veneered in thousands of perfectly fitted stone mosaic pieces forming grecas — geometric frets without a single repeating unit in some panels.

works.title: Xochicalco
works.meta: Epi-classic · c. 650–900 CE · Morelos, Mexico
works.desc: A fortified mountaintop city that hosted what may have been an international calendar congress, depicted on the Pyramid of the Feathered Serpent's beautifully carved reliefs. An underground observatory tracks the sun through a vertical shaft at zenith passage.

works.title: Templo Mayor
works.meta: Aztec · 1325–1521 · Tenochtitlan
works.desc: Dual pyramid of Huitzilopochtli and Tlaloc, discovered beneath downtown Mexico City in 1978 when utility workers struck the Coyolxauhqui Stone. Seven construction phases climb one over the other inside the exposed ruin.

works.title: Caral
works.meta: Norte Chico · c. 2600 BCE · Peru
works.desc: A city of six pyramids and sunken plazas older than the pyramids of Giza. Without pottery, ceramics, or writing, its builders sustained a monumental civilization on irrigation and anchovy fishing from Peru's central coast.

works.title: Raimondi Stele
works.meta: Chavín · c. 500 BCE · Peru
works.desc: A two-meter granite slab carved with the Staff God, his elaborate headdress readable in more than one direction — right-side up he is a fanged deity, upside down a different snarling face emerges from the headdress. The ambiguity is programmatic.

works.title: Huaca de la Luna
works.meta: Moche · c. 100–800 CE · Peru
works.desc: A staggered adobe pyramid rebuilt in six successive phases, each burying the previous in a deliberate act of renewal. Inner walls were painted with giant Ai Apaec decapitator gods in red, white, yellow, and black — pigments that survived because each phase sealed the previous art beneath fresh bricks.

works.title: Kuelap
works.meta: Chachapoya · 6th–16th century · Peru
works.desc: A cloud-forest citadel enclosed by twenty-meter limestone walls, sheltering over 400 round stone houses on a ridge 3,000 meters above sea level. The Chachapoya held out longer than the Inca against Spanish conquest thanks to sheer isolation.

works.title: Coricancha
works.meta: Inca · c. 1200–1500 · Cuzco
works.desc: The Inca 'Golden Enclosure,' a Sun temple whose walls were once sheathed in gold plate stripped by the Spanish for ransom. The surviving trapezoidal masonry — so precise the joints cannot be penetrated by a knife blade — now supports the colonial Church of Santo Domingo.

works.title: Pueblo Bonito
works.meta: Ancestral Puebloan · 9th–12th century · Chaco Canyon
works.desc: A 'great house' of six hundred rooms and forty ceremonial kivas arranged in a half-D around a central plaza. Its T-shaped doors align with lunar standstills; ancient roads radiate outward across the San Juan Basin for hundreds of kilometers.

works.title: Moundville
works.meta: Mississippian · 1000–1450 CE · Alabama
works.desc: A 300-acre ceremonial center with twenty-nine earthen platform mounds around a plaza, once home to about a thousand people. Moundville engravers produced copper plates of the Birdman and hand-and-eye motifs that defined the Southeastern Ceremonial Complex.

works.title: Canyon de Chelly
works.meta: Ancestral Puebloan · 350–1300 CE · Arizona
works.desc: Red sandstone canyon with cliff-dweller ruins tucked into shadowed overhangs. Later a Navajo stronghold; Kit Carson's 1864 campaign forced the Long Walk from here.

works.title: Olmec Wrestler
works.meta: Olmec · c. 1000–400 BCE · Mexico
works.desc: A squat basalt figure of a bearded, bare-chested man in a fighting crouch. A rare free-standing Olmec sculpture — the colossal heads sit on the ground, but this one coils like it could stand up.

works.title: Sarcophagus Lid of Pakal
works.meta: Classic Maya · 683 CE · Palenque
works.desc: Carved limestone lid over the tomb of K'inich Janaab' Pakal inside the Temple of the Inscriptions. Pakal falls into the jaws of the underworld at the base of the world-tree — or, to von Däniken, drives a spaceship.

works.title: Raven and the First Men
works.meta: Contemporary Haida · 1980
works.desc: Bill Reid's 4-ton yellow cedar sculpture of Raven discovering the first humans huddled inside a clamshell. Haida cosmology carved in a museum rotunda; an emblem of Indigenous art's modern presence.

works.title: Casa Grande Ruins
works.meta: Hohokam · c. 1350 · Arizona
works.desc: A four-story "great house" of caliche mud in the Sonoran Desert — the largest prehistoric structure in the American Southwest. Aligned to solstice and lunar events; abandoned before the Spanish arrived.

works.title: Wari Tapestry Tunic
works.meta: Wari · 7th–11th c. · Peru
works.desc: A tightly woven alpaca-wool tunic covered in flattened, abstracted figures and geometric grids. Wari weavers worked at more than 120 weft threads per inch — finer than most European tapestry.

works.title: Lord of Las Limas
works.meta: Olmec · c. 900 BCE · Mexico
works.desc: A greenstone sculpture of an adolescent Olmec youth holding a were-jaguar infant on his lap — found complete by two children playing on a hill in Veracruz in 1965.

works.title: Feathered Serpent Pyramid, Teotihuacan
works.meta: Teotihuacan · 2nd c. CE · Mexico
works.desc: Third-largest pyramid at the site, facade covered in 260 snake heads alternating with headdress gods — Quetzalcoatl avant la lettre. Built over a mass sacrificial burial of warriors.

works.title: Yaxchilán Lintel 24
works.meta: Classic Maya · 725 CE · Mexico
works.desc: Lady Xoc draws a rope studded with thorns through her tongue; her husband Shield Jaguar holds a torch over her. A bloodletting ritual carved in limestone with breathtaking fineness.

works.title: Maya Polychrome Vessel
works.meta: Late Classic Maya · 7th–9th c. CE
works.desc: A cylindrical vase painted with court scenes, Underworld journeys, or god-dialogues in red-orange slips. Maya "codex-style" vases are lost books — texts that outlived the paper they were once copied onto.

works.title: Jaina Island Figurine
works.meta: Late Classic Maya · 7th–9th c. CE
works.desc: Small ceramic figures of warriors, weavers, ballplayers, elite women — excavated from graves on Jaina, a tiny mortuary island off the Campeche coast. Among the liveliest small sculpture the Americas produced.

works.title: Codex Zouche-Nuttall
works.meta: Mixtec · 14th c. · Mexico
works.desc: A painted deer-hide screenfold codex reading back-and-forth across panels — the genealogies of the Mixtec kingdom, Lord Eight Deer conquering his rivals. One of the few surviving pre-conquest Mesoamerican books.

works.title: Diquís Stone Spheres
works.meta: Diquís · 600–1500 CE · Costa Rica
works.desc: Over 300 near-perfect stone spheres up to 2.5 meters across, scattered across the Diquís delta. Made by pecking and polishing gabbro boulders — purpose still debated.

works.title: Chavín Lanzón
works.meta: Chavín · c. 900 BCE · Peru
works.desc: A 4.5-meter granite spear, carved with a fanged anthropomorphic deity, rising through the floor of a small chamber at the center of Chavín de Huántar. The Andean Pantocrator — still rooted in situ.

works.title: Moche Portrait Vessel
works.meta: Moche · 100 BCE–500 CE · Peru
works.desc: A stirrup-spout ceramic of an individual man — scarred, aging, recognizably alive. Moche potters made dozens of portraits of the same ruler across his lifetime: a visual biography in clay.

works.title: Nazca Polychrome Vessel
works.meta: Nazca · 100 BCE–800 CE · Peru
works.desc: Thin-walled clay bowls painted with anthropomorphic fishermen, trophy heads, stylized creatures — the same visual vocabulary as the giant desert Lines, reduced to pot scale.

works.title: Gate of the Sun
works.meta: Tiwanaku · c. 500 CE · Bolivia
works.desc: A monolithic basalt gateway with a central "Staff God" figure carved above the doorway, attended by rays of forty winged figures. Broken into three pieces in antiquity; reassembled in situ.

works.title: Moray Agricultural Terraces
works.meta: Inca · 15th c. · Peru
works.desc: Concentric circular terraces sunk into the earth — each ring a different microclimate, temperatures ranging 15°C between top and bottom. The Inca agricultural laboratory, where crops were tested for alpine adaptation.

works.title: Cuzco School Painting
works.meta: Colonial Andean · 17th–18th c. · Peru
works.desc: Indigenous and mestizo painters trained by Spanish religious, painting Virgins in tiered skirts that double as mountains (Pachamama), archangels in lace collars carrying muskets. Baroque European form seeded in Andean soil.

works.title: Feather Headdress of Moctezuma
works.meta: Aztec · c. 1515 · Mexico
works.desc: Four hundred quetzal feathers and gold discs mounted on a fiber base — long enough to reach a man's heels. Sent from Moctezuma (or his successor) to Charles V, now held in Vienna; Mexican repatriation requests have not yet succeeded.

works.title: Mixtec Gold of Tomb 7
works.meta: Mixtec · 14th c. · Monte Albán
works.desc: 500+ pieces of filigree-cast gold — pectorals, jaw-ornaments, rings — found in a reused Zapotec tomb by Alfonso Caso in 1932. A glimpse of Mesoamerican goldwork largely melted down after the Conquest.

works.title: Inca Miniature Llama
works.meta: Inca · 15th c. · Peru
works.desc: Small silver or gold llamas, hollow, hollow-cast — buried in the highest-altitude shrines of the empire as offerings. Each one meticulous to the ear-tassel despite being smaller than a finger.

works.title: Detroit Industry Murals
works.meta: Mexican muralism · 1933
works.desc: Twenty-seven fresco panels wrapping the inner court of the Detroit Institute of Arts — Rivera's homage to the Rouge auto factory, workers at the assembly line deified like Aztec labor gods.

works.title: Prometheus (Pomona College)
works.meta: Mexican muralism · 1930
works.desc: A towering Prometheus bringing fire into Frary Hall, Pomona College. The first significant Mexican mural commissioned in the United States — and the doorway for a wave of muralists who followed.

works.title: Sandías (Tamayo)
works.meta: Mexican modernist · mid-20th c.
works.desc: Slabs of crimson watermelon arranged as if for a sacrificial altar — Rufino Tamayo returned to the same subject for decades, turning a peasant still life into national emblem.

works.title: Tlaltecuhtli Monolith
works.meta: Aztec · c. 1500 · Tenochtitlan
works.desc: A pink andesite slab of the Aztec earth-goddess, mouth open, hair writhing with skulls — found in 2006 at Templo Mayor, face down in a ritual burial. 4 meters long, the largest Aztec monolith ever found.

works.title: Olmec Celt-Pendant
works.meta: Olmec · 900–600 BCE · Mexico
works.desc: A polished jade celt — an axe-like form — incised with a were-jaguar mask. Olmec celts were worn as pendants but derived their form from the sharpened agricultural tools of the period.

works.title: Maya Whistling Vessel
works.meta: Early Classic Maya · 400–500 CE
works.desc: A ceramic vessel shaped as a seated figure — when you tip water out, air passing through a hidden chamber makes the figure whistle. Ritual technology in fired clay.

works.title: Moche Mountain Sacrifice Bottle
works.meta: Moche · 500–800 CE · Peru
works.desc: A stirrup-spout ceramic modeled into a small mountain with figures being sacrificed at its peaks — a portable vision of the Andean concept that gods lived atop peaks and demanded offerings there.

works.title: Nayarit House Model
works.meta: West Mexico · 200 BCE–300 CE
works.desc: A ceramic cross-section of a family compound — courtyards, musicians on the roof, a figure sleeping inside. Nayarit tomb models preserve an ethnographic snapshot of a culture with no written record.

works.title: Andean Processional Cross
works.meta: Colonial Andean · 14th–15th c. attributions contested · Ecuador
works.desc: A silver processional cross worked with Inca-era techniques for a Catholic rite — indigenous silversmiths picking up imported iconography within an unbroken Andean metalworking tradition.

works.title: Maya Censer with Seated King
works.meta: Early Classic Maya · 300–400 CE
works.desc: A ceramic censer with a seated king modeled on its lid, his body a hollow for burning copal incense. Smoke rose literally through the figure — embodying divine breath.

works.title: Maya Mirror-Bearer
works.meta: Classic Maya · 410–650 CE
works.desc: A kneeling court dwarf, arms raised to support a disk of pyrite mirror — the mirror-bearer, a standard position of the Maya court. Status made visible in the carrying.

works.title: Relief of Enthroned Ruler (Chakalte')
works.meta: Classic Maya · 770 CE
works.desc: A limestone wall relief of a seated king before an attendant, carved and signed by the sculptor Chakalte' — one of the few Maya artists whose name we know, in a culture where authorship was sometimes acknowledged.

works.title: Chimú Gold Collar
works.meta: Chimú · 1000–1470 CE · Peru
works.desc: A broad gold collar from the kingdom of Chan Chan — the Chimú worked more gold than anyone in the pre-Columbian Americas. Much was melted down after the Spanish conquest; a fraction survives.

works.title: Aztec Maize Carrier
works.meta: Aztec · 14th–16th c. · Central Mexico
works.desc: A stone seated male clutching ears of corn — an agricultural offering made concrete, maize as proxy for the god who gave it. Smaller and more intimate than the great Templo Mayor sculptures.

works.title: Aztec Gold Warrior
works.meta: Aztec · 14th–16th c. · Tetzcoco
works.desc: A cast gold-silver-copper figure of an armed warrior. Most Aztec metalwork was melted down by the Spanish in 1521; lost-wax gold figures like this one are staggeringly rare.

works.title: Macuilxochitl (Aztec Seated Deity)
works.meta: Aztec · 14th–16th c.
works.desc: A volcanic-stone deity, possibly Macuilxochitl ("Five Flower"), god of music, games, and excess, red pigment still clinging to the folds of his kilt. One of many minor Aztec gods with specialized domains.

works.title: Paracas Mantle
works.meta: Paracas · 300 BCE–200 CE · Peru
works.desc: A full camelid-wool mantle embroidered with tiny figures in repeating rows — some holding trophy heads. Preserved whole in the desert bundles of the Paracas necropolis.

works.title: Veracruz Palma
works.meta: Classic Veracruz · 800–1100 CE · Mexico
works.desc: A tall, flat stone "palma" carved in relief with a figure — worn at the waist as part of Mesoamerican ballgame regalia, symbolic gear rather than literal protective equipment.

works.title: Veracruz Disk
works.meta: Classic Veracruz · 600–900 CE · Mexico
works.desc: A slate disk with cinnabar-painted relief — probably a mirror-back, worn at the chest as regalia. The iconography mixes Maya imagery with local Veracruz scroll patterns.

works.title: Veracruz Fish Hacha
works.meta: Classic Veracruz · 600–900 CE · Mexico
works.desc: An "axe-form" (hacha) shaped as a fish-head — ballgame regalia worn in the belt. Traces of original plaster and pigment still cling; hachas were as much costume as sport gear.

works.title: Nasca Trophy-Head Bowl
works.meta: Nasca · 100–650 CE · Peru
works.desc: A polychrome ceramic bowl ringed with rows of severed trophy heads — the Nasca kept actual embalmed heads on cords, carried into ritual. The bowls depicted what the rites required.

works.title: Nasca Male Effigy Vessel
works.meta: Nasca · 100–650 CE · Peru
works.desc: A stirrup-spout vessel shaped as a seated man — painted face, closed eyes, carrying a cup. Nasca pottery matched its fine textiles in color range, unique in pre-Columbian Peru.

works.title: Nasca Gold Mouth Mask
works.meta: Nasca · 100 BCE–700 CE · Peru
works.desc: A hammered gold mask with tentacle-like projections — worn in the mouth or nostrils at ritual moments. Gold meant "the sweat of the sun" in Andean cosmology.

works.title: Wari Sacrificer Container
works.meta: Wari · c. 800 CE · Andes
works.desc: A carved wooden vessel of a Wari sacrificer — trophy head in hand, his own face echoing his victim's. The Wari empire stretched south from Ayacucho to Cuzco, preceding the Inca.

works.title: Northwest Coast Feast Ladle
works.meta: Northwest Coast · late 19th c.
works.desc: A carved wooden ladle for serving communal food at potlatches — the handle a bird or sea-creature, its beak curling up over the bowl. Prestige measured in generosity.

works.title: Northwest Coast Mask
works.meta: Northwest Coast · late 19th c.
works.desc: A carved and painted wooden mask — clan crest or shamanic being. Northwest Coast masks often had inner and outer faces; the outer opened to reveal the inner through strings the dancer pulled.

works.title: Tairona Double-Bat Bowl
works.meta: Tairona · 10th–16th c. · Colombia
works.desc: A ceramic bowl with twin bat effigies on its rim — the Tairona, ancestors of today's Kogi, made dense zoomorphic pottery that read as sculpture more than tableware.

works.title: Tairona Bird Pendant
works.meta: Tairona · 10th–16th c. · Colombia
works.desc: A cast tumbaga (gold-copper alloy) bird pendant — Tairona metalwork was lost-wax cast and polished to a warm gold surface. Worn at the breast of chiefs.

works.title: Inca Half Tunic
works.meta: Inca · 16th c. · Peru
works.desc: Half of an Inca unku — tightly woven tapestry with tocapu (small geometric blocks) that encoded social status, ethnic affiliation, or administrative role in ways still imperfectly understood.

works.title: Inca Checkerboard Tunic
works.meta: Inca · 15th–16th c. · Peru
works.desc: A full camelid-wool tunic in repeating black-and-white squares — the standard uniform of Inca military officers, recognizable at a distance on the battlefield.

works.title: Ica Fragmentary Mantle
works.meta: Ica Valley · 15th c. · Peru
works.desc: A textile fragment from the Ica Valley — the coastal successor culture to Paracas and Nasca, still producing complex figural weavings on the eve of Spanish arrival.

works.title: Tlingit Chilkat Blanket
works.meta: Tlingit · Northwest Coast · late 19th c. · cedar bark and mountain goat
works.desc: A ceremonial dance robe of cedar bark and mountain-goat wool — Chilkat weaving draws its formline crest figures freehand rather than on a grid, almost unique among loom traditions.

works.title: Tlingit Totem Pole
works.meta: Tlingit · Northwest Coast · c. 1880 · carved and painted cedar
works.desc: A Tlingit cedar totem pole — not religion or writing but lineage heraldry, declaring a clan house's crest animals. Most surviving 19th-century poles were cut down and sold; few stand in place.

works.title: Calima Gold Pectoral
works.meta: Calima · Colombia · 1–800 CE · hammered gold
works.desc: A hammered-gold chest ornament worn across the collarbone at full width — Calima lords in the Cauca Valley dressed in pounded gold heavy enough to have been audible as they moved.

works.title: Sinú Gold Bird Finial
works.meta: Sinú (Zenú) · Colombia · 5th–11th c. · cast gold
works.desc: A cast-gold finial shaped as a long-beaked bird — the Sinú crowned staffs and litter-poles with these, their lost-wax casting so refined the Spanish melted most of it down for bullion.

works.title: Aztec Tlaloc
works.meta: Aztec · Central Mexico · 13th–16th c. · stone
works.desc: The rain god with his goggle eyes and fanged mouth — Tenochtitlán sat on a lake-surrounded island, and the Aztecs owed their two harvests a year to Tlaloc and his dwarf rain-helpers.

works.title: Mimbres Bowl
works.meta: Mimbres · New Mexico · c. 1000–1150 · painted earthenware
works.desc: A black-on-white bowl buried with the dead, its base punctured to release the spirit. The Mimbres abandoned their valley around 1150 and the painting tradition stopped with them.
